retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Occlusal Contact Relations And Mandibular Movements -- Structure and Function -- Maxillary and Mandibular Dental Arch
retrieved_docs.content: The permanent, succedaneous teeth in adults consist of 16 teeth in the maxilla and 16 teeth in the mandible. The universal numbering system for permanent teeth assigns numbers to each tooth from 1 to 32. Tooth #1 is the right maxillary third molar followed sequentially by numbers 2-15 across the maxilla until reaching the left maxillary third molar, tooth #16. Numbering continues down to the mandible, with tooth #17 being the left mandibular third molar followed by 18-31 proceeding sequentially across the mandible until reaching the right mandibular third molar, which is tooth #32. [7] [8] There are alternative numbering systems.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Occlusal Contact Relations And Mandibular Movements -- Structure and Function -- Dental Occlusal Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: The following 5 surfaces of the crown of a tooth allow for discussion of occlusal contacts: [8] Facial: oriented toward the lips or teeth and referred to as labial for incisors/canines or buccal for premolars/molars Palatal/Lingual: oriented towards the palate for the maxilla and the tongue for the mandible Mesial: oriented toward the midline Distal: oriented away from the midline Occlusal: oriented toward the upper or lower jaw depending on the mandible versus the maxilla

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Maxillary first molars typically have two buccal roots and one larger palatal root. [4] Roots may splay and have variable curvature. [4] Both the maxillary first and second molars have distal root inclinations, with the second molar often to a greater degree than the first. The second molar also traditionally has three roots that are usually less splayed than the first molar. [5] In some cases, the roots may fuse on the buccal aspect. The maxillary third molar has the most significant anatomical variability of all teeth. The roots of the maxillary third molar tend to be fused and shorter than the maxillary first and second molars. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Occlusal Contact Relations And Mandibular Movements -- Structure and Function -- Maxillary and Mandibular Dental Arch
retrieved_docs.content: Adult dentition consists of 2 central incisors, 2 lateral incisors, 2 canines, 2 first premolars, 2-second premolars, 2 first molars, 2-second molars, and 2 third molars, both in the maxilla and mandible. The teeth are secured within the alveolar ridges of the maxilla and mandible. Dentition, whether native or restored, is integral to masticatory function. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Nose Paranasal Sinuses -- Structure and Function -- Maxillary Sinus
retrieved_docs.content: The orbit floor forms the superior wall, the sinus roof. The infraorbital artery (branch of the maxillary artery) and nerve (branch of the maxillary division of the trigeminal nerve) run through this wall and enter the infraorbital groove. [9] The medial wall separates the maxillary sinus from the nasal cavity. Still, they communicate throughout the ostium, located in the medial wall inferior or at the same level of the orbit floor. [9] The inferior wall, known as the sinus floor, is closely related to the posterior teeth apices, from which it is separated only by a layer of compact bone. The mean distance between the dental apices and the sinus floor is 1.97 mm, [10] and the apices of the molars are closer to the sinus floor than the premolars. [10] The first molars have been found to perforate the sinus floor in 2.2% of cases, and the second molars in 2% of cases. The buccodistal root of the second molar is the closest to the maxillary sinus floor. [2] The maxillary sinuses frequently present septa, thin plates of cortical bone arising from the sinus floor, better visualized on cone-beam CT scans. Primary septa occur during the development of the sinus [11] , whereas secondary septa occur after tooth loss. [12] Apart from septa, tooth loss causes a local lowering of the sinus floor and alveolar bone resorption. [9] Mucus-producing ciliated pseudostratified columnar epithelium lines the maxillary sinus, which lacks periosteum. The cilia have the heavy task of draining the mucous into the ostium (located superiorly in the medial wall), and they are more numerous near the ostium. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Maxilla -- Structure and Function -- Maxillary Sinus
retrieved_docs.content: The roof of the sinus contributes to the orbital floor and houses the infraorbital canal, which transmits the infraorbital neurovascular bundle. This bundle exits the skull through the infraorbital foramen, located approximately 1 cm below the infraorbital rim. [13] The floor of the sinus lies just superior to the alveolar process and often approaches the roots of the molars. The lowest point is typically near the 1st molar. Vertical position varies with age, ranging from level with the nasal floor to approximately 1 cm below it.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Occlusal Contact Relations And Mandibular Movements -- Structure and Function -- Dental Occlusal Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: Tooth cusps function to guide erupting teeth into appropriate occlusion with periodontal stimulation informing neural reflexes for jaw closure and mastication [1] . The maxillary palatal cusps and the mandibular facial cusps comprise the centric cusps involved in centric occlusion (discussed below). In contrast, the maxillary buccal cusp and the mandibular lingual cusps guide the mandible during the lateral excursion.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: When divided on the mid-line sagittal plane, these teeth are organized into the right and left halves. Incisors and canines can be classified further as "anterior teeth" and molars and premolars as "posterior teeth." The primary teeth erupt during the first year and into the third years of life and are then exfoliated(shed) between the ages of 6 and 12. Since primary teeth are shed, as some trees shed their leaves, they are also referred to as deciduous teeth, which are eventually replaced by the adult permanent dentition. [2] Primary teeth are labeled in the following classification system and are designated in a clockwise direction: A: Right maxillary 2nd molar B: Right maxillary 1st molar C: Right maxillary canine D: Right maxillary lateral incisor E: Right maxillary central incisor F: Left maxillary central incisor G: Left maxillary lateral incisor H: Left maxillary canine I: Left maxillary 1st molar J: Left maxillary 2nd molar T: Right mandibular 2nd molar

retrieved_docs.title: Le Fort Osteotomy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Careful planning of the surgical incision is necessary to provide an adequate cuff of gingival tissue for soft tissue closure. A minimum of 5 mm above the mucogingival junction should be maintained. The initial mucosal surgical incision is made from the 1st molar to the first molar utilizing either a #15 blade or electrocautery perpendicular to the maxilla. The incision is extended down to the bone. The periosteum is scored to facilitate subperiosteal dissection, performed with a periosteal elevator medially to the piriform rims. Care is taken to dissect the nasal mucosa around the nasal aperture. A curved freer elevator or the blunt end of a Woodson elevator may assist with the dissection of the nasal mucosa.

retrieved_docs.title: Basilar Invagination -- Evaluation -- BI is most often diagnosed pertaining to the following midsagittal craniovertebral parameters:
retrieved_docs.content: Chamberlain's line-running from the posterior aspect of the hard palate and the posterior margin of the foramen magnum McGregor's line-running from the posterior margin of the hard palate to the lowest aspect of the squama occipitalis (as the posterior edge of the foramen magnum could not always be visualized) McRae's line – connotes the anteroposterior line at the foramen magnum [15] [17] The criteria for diagnosis are the tip of the dens lying >5 mm above Chamberlain's Line, >7 mm above McGregor's Line, or McRae's line.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Structure and Function -- Maxillary Primary Teeth
retrieved_docs.content: First Molars – The maxillary first molars (tooth B, I) morphologically resemble both a molar and a premolar. Its occlusal surface consists of the mesiobuccal, distolingual, mesiolingual, and distobuccal cusps. The largest cusp is the mesiolingual cusp, and it also represents the sharpest cusp. The distolingual cusp is small and rounded in morphology. The buccal surface is smooth, and the three roots are long and widely spread. Of note, the primary 1st molar has a less pronounced mesiobuccal cusp when compared to the permanent 1st molar.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Roots of posterior maxillary teeth commonly contact or protrude through the floor of the ipsilateral maxillary sinus. Maxillary molars demonstrated protrusion through the maxillary sinus in greater than 50% of cases, with the mesiobuccal root of maxillary second molars showing the most significant depth of protrusion of all maxillary posterior tooth roots. [30] Other anatomical areas of concern for root displacement are the submandibular, sublingual, buccal, and pterygomandibular spaces. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Maxilla -- Structure and Function -- Zygomatic Process
retrieved_docs.content: The zygomatic process projects laterally from the maxilla, forming the superolateral border of the maxillary sinus. This structure lies above the 1st maxillary molar and articulates inferiorly with the alveolar process and superomedially with the frontal process (see Image . Zygomatic Process of the Maxilla). The zygomatic process reinforces the structural integrity of the midface together with the alveolar process. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Posterior Crossbite -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In the transverse dimension, normal occlusion is when the palatine cusps of the upper molars and premolars occlude in the fossa of the lower molars and premolars. In the anteroposterior plane, the upper incisors occlude on the labial aspects of the lower incisors.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Introduction -- Classification of Malocclusion
retrieved_docs.content: Angle class I molar classification (also known as neutroclusion) is determined by the mesiobuccal cusp of the maxillary first molar occluding with the buccal groove of the mandibular first molar. A class II molar classification (mesoclusion) is determined by the mesiobuccal cusp of the maxillary first molar, occluding mesial to the buccal groove of the mandibular first molar. Lastly, a class III molar classification is determined by the mesiobuccal cusp of the maxillary first molar occluding distal to the buccal groove of the mandibular first molar (distoclusion). [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Basilar Invagination -- Evaluation -- Recently, BI has been divided into:
retrieved_docs.content: Group A BI- the tip of the odontoid process is above the Chamberlain line, McRae line, and Wackenheim's clival line. Group B BI- the odontoid process and clivus remained anatomically aligned, and the tip of the odontoid process is above Chamberlain's line but below McRae's and Wackenheim's lines. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 226
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are directly performed on zuby mléčné, indicating that primary (baby) teeth serve as the specific sites for these dental interventions.

retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 249
retrieved_docs.content: Dental procedures are performed directly on zuby mléčné (the primary or “milk” teeth), indicating that therapeutic dental interventions often target the primary dentition. Likewise, disorders of teeth and jaw are located in zuby mléčné, showing that many oral pathologies also affect these early‑life teeth. Together, these relationships emphasize that both clinical treatments and disease processes in pediatric dentistry are closely linked to the primary teeth, underscoring the importance of careful assessment and management of zuby mléčné in patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: Nasal endoscopy with maxillary antrostomy is classified as a diagnostic procedure involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx, providing clinicians with direct endoscopic visualization and access to the maxillary sinus for assessment of sinonasal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 243
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of teeth and jaw are classified under the broader parent category zuby and are specifically associated with the anatomical location of zuby mléčné, the primary (milk) teeth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a dental procedure that directly targets teeth (zuby) and is recognized as part of the broader set of dental procedures. Dental procedures themselves are also performed directly on deciduous teeth (zuby mléčné). Disorders of teeth and jaw are categorized under the parent concept of teeth (zuby) and are specifically located in the deciduous dentition (zuby mléčné). Consequently, both the extraction of a single tooth and other dental procedures may be applied to permanent and baby teeth, while disorders of the teeth and jaw primarily affect the baby teeth.

Recognizing this presentation is important for obstetric assessment and delivery planning, as it influences the choice of birth method and the management of potential complications.
